concepts.title: Slope of Tangent and Normal
concepts.conceptBody:
Tangents & Normal 

A very useful application of derivatives is slope of tangent and normal at a point on a curve.

Slope of Tangent: 

The slope of tangent to a continuous curve, $y=f\left( x \right)$, is given by

   

$\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ slope }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }=m={{\left. \frac{dy}{dx} \right|}_{P\left( x,y \right)}}$

at a point $\text{P}\left( {{\text{x}}_{1}},{{\text{y}}_{1}} \right)$ on the curve

From the diagram ${{\left( \frac{dy}{dx} \right)}_{P}}=\text{tan}\theta $

Tangent parallel to $x$ axis: then, ${{\left. \frac{dy}{dx} \right|}_{P}}=0$

Tangent perpendicular to axis: then ${{\left. \frac{dy}{dx} \right|}_{P}}$ approaches $\infty $ or ${{\left. \frac{dx}{dy} \right|}_{P}}=0$

 

Slope of Normal: 

We know normal & tangent are perpendicular to each other. Hence

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}}\text{ } \text{ Slope } \text{ of } \text{ normal } \text{ } &  =\frac{-1}{\text{ } \text{ slope } \text{ of } \text{ tangent } \text{ at } \text{ P}} \\{} &  =\frac{-1}{{{(\text{dy}/\text{dx})}_{\text{P}}}} \\{} &  =-{{\left( \frac{\text{dx}}{\text{dy}} \right)}_{\text{P}}} \\\end{array}$

concepts.title: Equation of Tangent and Normal
concepts.conceptBody:
Equation of Tangent & Normal 

Now since we know the slopes, finding the equation of tangents & normal at a point is really simple. As we have done it earlier also in chapter like Straight lines etc.

Equation of tangent at a point $\left( {{\text{x}}_{1}},{{\text{y}}_{1}} \right)\equiv \text{y}-{{\text{y}}_{1}}={{\left( \frac{\text{dy}}{\text{dx}} \right)}_{{{\text{x}}_{1}}{{\text{y}}_{1}}}}\left( \text{x}-{{\text{x}}_{1}} \right)$

Equation of normal at a point $\left( {{\text{x}}_{1}},{{\text{y}}_{1}} \right)\equiv \text{y}-{{\text{y}}_{1}}=-{{\left( \frac{\text{dx}}{\text{dy}} \right)}_{{{\text{x}}_{1}}{{\text{y}}_{1}}}}\left( \text{x}-{{\text{x}}_{1}} \right)$

concepts.title: Angle of Intersection of two curves
concepts.conceptBody:
Angle of Intersection of Curves 

Angle of intersection of 2 curves is defined as angle between the tangents to the curve at their point of intersection.

We require $\theta $, as it is the angle between the 2 curves.

$\theta ={{\theta }_{2}}-{{\theta }_{1}}$

also we know that $\text{tan}{{\theta }_{1}}\And \text{tan}{{\theta }_{2}}$ are the slopes of tangents to the curve ${{\text{C}}_{1}}\And {{\text{C}}_{2}}$ respectively at point $\text{P}\left( {{\text{x}}_{1}},{{\text{y}}_{1}} \right)$. taking tan of the previous equation

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}}\text{tan}\theta & ~=\text{tan}\left( {{\theta }_{2}}-{{\theta }_{1}} \right) \\{} & ~=\frac{\text{tan}{{\theta }_{2}}-\text{tan}{{\theta }_{1}}}{1+\text{tan}{{\theta }_{2}}\text{tan}\theta } \\{} & ~=\frac{{{\left( \frac{dy}{dx} \right)}_{{{C}_{1}}}}-{{\left( \frac{dy}{dx} \right)}_{\text{C}2}}}{1+{{\left( \frac{dy}{dx} \right)}_{C2}}{{\left( \frac{dy}{dx} \right)}_{{{C}_{1}}}}} \\\end{array}$

Generally we take acute angle as the angle of intersection. We will get 2 answers $\theta \And 180-\theta $, $\And $ we select the smaller of 2.

concepts.title: Length of Tangent to circle
concepts.conceptBody:
Length of Tangent 

I. The length of the tangent drawn from a given external point $\left( {{x}_{1}},{{y}_{1}} \right)$ to the circle ${{\text{x}}^{2}}+{{\text{y}}^{2}}={{\text{a}}^{2}}$ is given by $\sqrt{\text{x}_{1}^{2}+\text{y}_{1}^{2}-{{\text{a}}^{2}}}$ 

Proof: 

Equation of given circle is ${{\text{x}}^{2}}+{{\text{y}}^{2}}={{\text{a}}^{2}}$

Its Centre is $\text{C}\left( 0,0 \right)$ and radius is a. Let $\text{P}\left( {{\text{x}}_{1}},{{\text{y}}_{1}} \right)$ be the given point. From $\text{P}$ let $\text{PT}$ be a tangent to the circle touching the circle at T. Then PT will be the length of the tangent. Join $\text{C},\text{P}$ and $\text{C},\text{T}$ since $\text{PT}\bot \text{CT}$ hence from right angled $\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ CTP},\text{C}{{\text{T}}^{2}}+\text{P}{{\text{T}}^{2}}=\text{C}{{\text{P}}^{2}}$

or $\text{P}{{\text{T}}^{2}}=\text{C}{{\text{P}}^{2}}-\text{C}{{\text{T}}^{2}}$

But $\text{CT}=\text{ the }\text{ radius }\text{ of }\text{ the }\text{ circle }=\text{a}$

 

and $\text{C}{{\text{P}}^{2}}={{\left( {{\text{x}}_{1}}-0 \right)}^{2}}+{{\left( {{\text{y}}_{1}}-0 \right)}^{2}}=\text{x}_{1}^{2}+\text{y}_{1}^{2}$

Therefore, from (1) $\text{P}{{\text{T}}^{2}}=\text{x}_{1}^{2}+\text{y}_{1}^{2}-{{\text{a}}^{2}}$

or \(\mathrm{P T}=\sqrt{x_1^2+y_1^2-a^2}\)

This is the required length of the tangent drawn from an external point $\left( {{\text{x}}_{1}},{{\text{y}}_{1}} \right)$ to circle (1).

II. The length of the tangent drawn from a given external point $\left( {{x}_{1}},{{y}_{1}} \right)$ to the circle 

${{x}^{2}}+{{y}^{2}}+2gx+2fy+c=0$ given by $\sqrt{x_{1}^{2}+y_{1}^{2}+2g{{x}_{1}}+2f{{y}_{1}}+c}$ 

 

The equation of given circle is ${{x}^{2}}+{{y}^{2}}+2gx+2fy+c=0$

Its Centre is $\text{C}\left( -\text{g},-\text{f} \right)$ and radius is $\sqrt{{{\text{g}}^{2}}+{{\text{f}}^{2}}-\text{c}}$.

Let $\text{P}\left( {{\text{x}}_{1}},{{\text{y}}_{1}} \right)$ be the given point. From $\text{P}$ Let $\text{PT}$ be a tangent to the circle touching the circle at $\text{T}$. Then PT will be the length of the tangent. Join C, T and C, P. Since CT $\bot $ PT.

$\therefore $ from right angled $\vartriangle \text{CTP}$,

$\text{C}{{\text{T}}^{2}}+\text{P}{{\text{T}}^{2}}=\text{C}{{\text{P}}^{2}},\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ or }\!\!~\!\!\text{ P}{{\text{T}}^{2}}=\text{C}{{\text{P}}^{2}}-\text{C}{{\text{T}}^{2}}$

But CT $~=\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ the }\!\!~\!\!\text{ radius }\!\!~\!\!\text{ of }\!\!~\!\!\text{ the }\!\!~\!\!\text{ circle }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }=\sqrt{{{\text{g}}^{2}}+{{\text{f}}^{2}}-\text{c}}$

and $\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & {} \\ \text{C}{{\text{P}}^{2}} & ~={{\text{(}{{\text{x}}_{1}}-\left( -\text{g} \right)\text{ }\!\!\}\!\!\text{ }}^{2}}+{{\left\{ {{\text{y}}_{1}}-\left( -\text{f} \right) \right\}}^{2}}={{\left( {{\text{x}}_{1}}+\text{g} \right)}^{2}}+{{\left( {{\text{y}}_{1}}+\text{f} \right)}^{2}} \\ {} & ~=\text{x}_{1}^{2}+\text{y}_{1}^{2}+2\text{g}{{\text{x}}_{1}}+2\text{f}{{\text{y}}_{1}}+{{\text{g}}^{2}}+{{\text{f}}^{2}} \\ \end{array}$

Therefore, from (2), we have

$\text{P}{{\text{T}}^{2}}$ $~=\left( \text{x}_{1}^{2}+\text{y}_{1}^{2}+2\text{g}{{\text{x}}_{1}}+2\text{f}{{\text{y}}_{1}}+{{\text{g}}^{2}}+{{\text{f}}^{2}} \right)-\left( \sqrt{{{\left. {{\text{g}}^{2}}+{{\text{f}}^{2}}-\text{c} \right)}^{2}}} \right.$

$~=\text{x}_{1}^{2}+\text{y}_{1}^{2}+2\text{g}{{\text{x}}_{1}}+2\text{f}{{\text{y}}_{1}}+{{\text{g}}^{2}}+{{\text{f}}^{2}}-{{\text{g}}^{2}}-{{\text{f}}^{2}}+\text{c}$

$\Rightarrow $ PT$~=\sqrt{\text{x}_{1}^{2}+\text{y}_{1}^{2}+2\text{g}{{\text{x}}_{1}}+2\text{f}{{\text{y}}_{1}}+\text{c}}$

This is the required length of the tangent drawn from an external point $\left( {{\text{x}}_{1}},{{\text{y}}_{1}} \right)$ to the given circle (1).

Tip : If $S\equiv 0$ is the equation of given circle and ${{s}_{1}}\equiv 0$ is the equation achieved by butting $\left( \text{x},{{\text{y}}_{1}} \right)$ in $\text{s}$, then the length o tangent from $\left( {{\text{x}}_{1}}{{\text{y}}_{1}} \right)$ is given by $\sqrt{{{\text{S}}_{1}}}$.

concepts.title: Orthogonal Curves
concepts.conceptBody:
Orthogonal Curves 

If 2 curves intersect at right angles i.e. the angle of intersection of 2 curves is a right angle, then the two curves are said to be orthogonal curves.

i.e. the two tangents are perpendicular

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\left( \frac{\text{dy}}{\text{dx}} \right)}_{{{\text{C}}_{1}}}}{{\left( \frac{\text{dy}}{\text{dx}} \right)}_{{{\text{C}}_{2}}}}=-1$
